quotes.quote: It was to them that Prince Jacaerys turned, at the urging of his fool, vowing that any man who could master a dragon would be granted lands and riches and dubbed a knight.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Jacaerys's proclamation on Dragonstone)
quotes.note: The call that launches the Sowing — the load-bearing inciting line for the whole event.

quotes.quote: Munkun's True Telling tells us that sixteen men lost their lives during the Sowing. Three times that number were burned or maimed.
quotes.attribution: Grand Maester Munkun's True Telling, via Gyldayn
quotes.note: States the cost of the Sowing in hard numbers — needed for the node's 'heavy cost' claim.

quotes.quote: Steffon Darklyn was burned to death whilst attempting to mount the dragon Seasmoke. Lord Gormon Massey suffered the same fate when approaching Vermithor.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (casualty roll of the Sowing)
quotes.note: Names two named casualties, grounding the abstract death toll in specific people.

quotes.quote: it ends with Mushroom running across the ward of Dragonstone with the seat of his pantaloons on fire, and nigh drowning when he leapt into a well to quench the flames.
quotes.attribution: Mushroom's Testimony, via Gyldayn
quotes.note: The 'ghastly business' had a farcical edge too — Mushroom's own botched attempt on Silverwing.

quotes.quote: When it came, the fall of Dragonstone took less than an hour.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn
quotes.note: States the event's defining fact — the speed of the coup — in one clean sentence.

quotes.quote: As Quince struggled to rise from his bed, Broome drove a spear into his huge pale belly.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Alfred Broome's betrayal)
quotes.note: The killing of castellan Robert Quince, the inside-job betrayal at the heart of the coup.

quotes.quote: King Aegon had leapt from the saddle when the dragons were still twenty feet from the ground, shattering both legs.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (aftermath of the Baela/Moondancer duel)
quotes.note: The price Aegon II paid to retake the island — sets up his permanent injury.

quotes.quote: And Aegon II lived the rest of his life in great pain...though to his honor, when Grand Maester Gerardys offered him milk of the poppy, he refused. “I shall not walk that road again,” he said.
quotes.attribution: Aegon II Targaryen, quoted in Gyldayn's chronicle
quotes.note: Aegon's own words refusing the poppy, tying his post-Rook's-Rest addiction fear to the fall of Dragonstone.

quotes.quote: Yet Rhaenyra knew him at once, and said, “Dear brother. | had hoped that you were dead.” “After you,” Aegon answered. “You are the elder.”
quotes.attribution: dialogue between Rhaenyra and Aegon II, chronicled by Gyldayn (the "|" reflects an OCR misread of "I" in the source scan)
quotes.note: The last exchange between the two rival monarchs, moments before her death.

quotes.quote: “If they search the seven hells, mayhaps,” the king made answer, as his men tore Rhaenyra from her son's arms.
quotes.attribution: Aegon II Targaryen, quoted in Gyldayn's chronicle
quotes.note: Aegon's answer when Rhaenyra warns her lords will find her — the moment her capture turns fatal.

quotes.quote: The smell of blood roused the dragon, who sniffed at Her Grace, then bathed her in a blast of flame, so suddenly that Ser Alfred's cloak caught fire as he leapt away.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn
quotes.note: The mechanics of the killing — Sunfyre roused by Broome pricking her breast with a dagger.

quotes.quote: upon her halfbrother before Sunfyre’s jaws closed round her, tearing off her arm and shoulder.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn
quotes.note: Line-wrap continuation of the sentence beginning on line 301 ('...shriek out one last curse') — the death blow itself.

quotes.quote: Vhagar was five times the size of her foe, the hardened survivor of a hundred battles. If there was a fight, it could not have lasted long.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn
quotes.note: Establishes the mismatch between Vhagar and young Arrax over Shipbreaker Bay.

quotes.quote: Arrax fell, broken, to be swallowed by the storm-lashed waters of the bay.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn
quotes.note: The moment of the kill, stated plainly.

quotes.quote: Lucerys Velaryon died with his dragon, Munkun insists. This is undoubtedly correct. The prince was thirteen years of age. His body was never found.
quotes.attribution: Grand Maester Munkun's True Telling, endorsed by Gyldayn
quotes.note: Gyldayn's own verdict cutting through the wilder rumor-tales (survival, mutilation, etc.) — the canonical fact of Luke's death.

quotes.quote: “An eye for an eye, a son for a son,” Prince Daemon wrote. “Lucerys shall be avenged.”
quotes.attribution: Prince Daemon Targaryen, raven from Harrenhal, quoted in Gyldayn's chronicle
quotes.note: Daemon's vow of vengeance for Luke's death — the direct causal seed of the Jaehaerys murder.

quotes.quote: Long simmering, the conflict burst into the open on the third day of the third moon of 129 AC, when the ailing, bedridden King Viserys | Targaryen closed his eyes for a nap in the Red Keep of King’s Landing and died without waking.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (the "|" reflects an OCR misread of the Roman numeral "I" in the source scan)
quotes.note: The core fact and exact date of Viserys's death, framed explicitly as the trigger for the Dance.

quotes.quote: The dwarf Mushroom suggests a more sinister scenario, whereby Queen Alicent hurried King Viserys on his way with a pinch of poison in his hippocras.
quotes.attribution: Mushroom's Testimony, via Gyldayn
quotes.note: The murder-vs-natural-death dispute that shadows the whole succession crisis.

quotes.quote: Neither septons nor silent sisters were summoned to the bedchamber where King Viserys lay, swollen and rotting.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn
quotes.note: The greens' concealment of the king's death while they lined up support before proclaiming Aegon.

quotes.quote: riders went forth on pale horses to spread the word to the people of King's Landing, crying “King Viserys is dead, long live King Aegon.”
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn
quotes.note: The public proclamation, ten days after the death, that ends the concealment and opens the succession war.

quotes.quote: The next two years were the years of the Dragon’s Wroth. Every castle in Dorne, save Sunspear, was burned thrice over, as Balerion and Vhagar returned time and time again.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn
quotes.note: The naming line for the event and its core claim (every castle but Sunspear burned thrice).

quotes.quote: Lord Fowler, Lord Vaith, Lady Toland, and four successive Lords of the Hellholt were murdered, one after the other, for the Iron Throne had offered a lord's ransom in gold for the head of any Dornish lord.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn
quotes.note: The bounty-on-Dornish-lords' heads detail named in the node's Identity text.

quotes.quote: Lord Connington of Griffin's Roost was killed whilst hunting, Lord Mertyns of Mistwood poisoned with his whole household by a cask of Dornish wine, Lord Fell smothered in a brothel in King's Landing.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn
quotes.note: The Dornish reprisal assassinations answering the bounties — the tit-for-tat structure of the Wroth.

quotes.quote: Admitted through a postern gate by a treacherous servant, the Wyl attackers slew Lord Oakheart and most of the wedding guests, then made the bride look on as they gelded her husband.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn
quotes.note: The Cafferen-Oakheart wedding massacre of 12 AC named explicitly in the node's Identity text.

quotes.quote: “Scream and you all die,” Blood told Her Grace. Queen Helaena kept her calm, it is said. “Who are you?” she demanded of the two. “Debt collectors,” said Cheese.
quotes.attribution: Blood and Cheese, quoted in Gyldayn's chronicle
quotes.note: The opening of the confrontation in Queen Alicent's chambers.

quotes.quote: “An eye for an eye, a son for a son. We only want the one, t’ square things.
quotes.attribution: Cheese, quoted in Gyldayn's chronicle
quotes.note: Cheese echoing Daemon's own vow (see W3A-16) while demanding the queen choose which son dies; quote ends mid-line where the source hard-wraps (dialogue continues onto line 271) so no closing quotation mark is present in the source at this point.

quotes.quote: “A wife's not ason,” said Blood. “It has to be a boy.”
quotes.attribution: Blood, quoted in Gyldayn's chronicle
quotes.note: Refusing Helaena's offer to die in her son's place. 'ason' is verbatim as printed in the source (an OCR/typesetting run-on of 'a son') — copied byte-exact, not corrected.

quotes.quote: Then he gave Blood a grin, and the hulking swordsman slew Prince Jaehaerys, striking off the boy’s head with a single blow.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn
quotes.note: The killing blow itself — the load-bearing fact of the node.

quotes.quote: Breakbones, as he was called, was heir to Harrenhal, and said to be the strongest man in the Seven Kingdoms.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Establishes his epithet, house standing, and reputation as the realm's strongest man.

quotes.quote: would leave her husband with his lover on such nights, and seek her own solace in the arms of Harwin Strong.
quotes.attribution: Mushroom's account, per Gyldayn's narration
quotes.note: The affair-with-Rhaenyra claim underpinning the paternity scandal and his eventual death.

quotes.quote: Amongst the greens, it was an article of faith that the father of Rhaenyra’s sons was not her husband, Laenor, but her champion, Harwin Strong.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: States the greens' core accusation against him directly and concisely.

quotes.quote: A big man, burly and balding, Lord Strong enjoyed a formidable reputation as a battler.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Physical description + reputation, core identity line.

quotes.quote: Lord Strong had been the King’s Hand, and Viserys had come to rely upon his strength and counsel.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Establishes his standing as Viserys's trusted Hand, distinct from the existing wiki 'hector me' quote.

quotes.quote: Laenor had the aquiline nose, silver-white hair, and purple eyes that bespoke his Valyrian blood.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Physical/Valyrian-blood description, not already among the node's existing wiki Quotes.

quotes.quote: Ser Laenor Velaryon, husband to the Princess Rhaenyra and the putative father of her children, was slain whilst attending a fair in Spicetown, stabbed to death by his friend and companion Ser Qarl Correy.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Concise identity-plus-death summary: husband, putative father, manner of death.

quotes.quote: the eldest of them, Ser Vaemond Velaryon, protested that the inheritance by rights should pass to him...on the grounds that Rhaenyra’s sons were bastards sired by Harwin Strong.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: States his claim and the bastardy accusation that got him executed.

quotes.quote: This rebellion cost him his head, but he left a wife and sons behind.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Terse summary of his fate, referenced retrospectively during his sons' Driftmark claim.

quotes.quote: Jason Lannister had assembled a formidable host in the western hills; a thousand armored knights, and seven times as many archers and men-at-arms.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Establishes the scale of the western host he led for the greens.

quotes.quote: in the last attempt, Lord Jason was dealt a mortal wound at the hand of a grizzled squire, Pate of Longleaf.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: His death at the Battle at the Red Fork, killed by a squire.

quotes.quote: In the west, Ser Jason Lannister and his twin, Ser Tyland, vied for her during a feast at Casterly Rock.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Establishes his earlier suit for Rhaenyra's hand alongside his twin Tyland.

quotes.quote: Never wed, Lady Jeyne had reigned over the Vale since the death of her father and elder brothers at the hands of the Stone Crows of the hills when she was three.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Core biographical identity line: epithet, age gap with Jacaerys, origin of her rule. New line-cited material beyond the node's existing wiki Quotes (which are all direct speech).

quotes.quote: Lady Jeyne Arryn, the Maiden of the Vale, died at Gulltown of a cold that had settled in her chest.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Her death notice, narrator voice, distinct from the node's existing speech quotes.

quotes.quote: At seven, she became a dragonrider, taking to the sky on the young dragon she named Syrax, after a goddess of old Valyria.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Origin of the dragon's name and Rhaenyra's bond with her.

quotes.quote: Syrax was the queen’s dragon. She had never known another rider.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Core one-rider-only identity fact, matches node's own Identity line.

quotes.quote: Syrax fell.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Terse standalone line marking her death at the Storming of the Dragonpit.

quotes.quote: Seasmoke, the pale grey beast that had been the pride and passion of Ser Laenor Velaryon
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Names his coloring and his bond with original rider Laenor; matches node's own phrasing.

quotes.quote: Seasmoke was first to die, when Vermithor locked his teeth into his neck and ripped his head off.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: His death at Second Tumbleton, killed by Vermithor.

quotes.quote: the oldest and largest living dragon in all Westeros was Vermithor, once the mount of the Old King, now that of Hard Hugh the bastard.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Establishes his stature and rider lineage (Old King to Hugh Hammer) in one line.

quotes.quote: Almost a hundred years old and as large as the two young dragons put together, the bronze dragon with the great tan wings was in a rage as he took flight, with blood smoking from a dozen wounds.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Vivid description of his age, size, and rage after being unhorsed at Second Tumbleton.

quotes.quote: Moondancer was a young dragon, pale green, with horns and crest and wingbones of pearl.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Core physical-description identity line, matches node's own Identity phrasing.

quotes.quote: On the ground, Moondancer's quickness proved of little use against Sunfyre’s size and weight.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: The turning point of her fatal duel with Sunfyre.

quotes.quote: Snow and ice and cold made Vermax ill-tempered, it is said,
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: A personality/temperament detail (dislikes cold), explaining why Jacaerys cut short his Winterfell stay.

quotes.quote: His flight jerked to a violent end, Vermax went down smoking and screaming, clawing at the water.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: His death at the Battle in the Gullet.

quotes.quote: When the ship heeled over and sank, Vermax sank with her.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Final line of his death, same paragraph as W3B-25.

quotes.quote: Vhagar was five times the size of her foe, the hardened survivor of a hundred battles.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Establishes the size mismatch that doomed Arrax against Vhagar.

quotes.quote: Arrax fell, broken, to be swallowed by the storm-lashed waters of the bay.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: His death over Shipbreaker Bay, taking Lucerys with him.

quotes.quote: Grey Ghost dwelt in a smoking vent high on the eastern side of the Dragonmont, preferred fish, and was most oft glimpsed flying low over the narrow sea, snatching prey from the waters.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Core habitat/diet identity line, matches node's own Identity phrasing.

quotes.quote: The dragon lay in two pieces, and had been torn apart and partially devoured.
quotes.attribution: chronicle of Archmaester Gyldayn (Fire & Blood narration)
quotes.note: Discovery of his corpse, killed by Sunfyre (long misattributed to the Cannibal).
